topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The unexpected bouquet landed on my cluttered kitchen counter with a soft thud. A riot of deep crimson roses and pale, almost sickly yellow lilies. I stared at them, the vibrant colors a jarring contrast to the grey monotony of my apartment. My jaw tightened, and I picked up a wilting stem, turning it over in my fingers as if searching for a hidden flaw. There was no note, just the weight of the petals and the silent, baffling gesture. I pushed the flowers aside, the movement jerky, and retreated to the living room, sinking onto the worn sofa. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, defeated weight settling onto my spine. I didn't look up when the doorbell rang again, the sound grating on my nerves.

[story 2]

I found the arrangement tucked beside my laptop, amidst the scattered papers and empty coffee mugs. The arrangement was offensively cheerful—bright orange tulips and a handful of stiff, white daisies. I didn't move for a full minute, just letting my gaze drift over the arrangement, cataloging its absurdity. A low, almost inaudible sigh escaped my lips, a sound heavy with disinterest. I finally reached out, my fingers brushing against a velvety petal. It felt alien and overly saccharine. I didn't feel gratitude; only a profound sense of being interrupted. I shoved the flowers into a vase on the windowsill, the glass rattling slightly, and went back to staring blankly at the screen, the silence of my room amplifying the dull thrum of my dissatisfaction.

[story 3]

When I opened the door, the vibrant splash of purple and deep blue against the muted hallway seemed to demand attention. A cluster of exotic orchids, impossibly perfect, rested on the welcome mat. I hesitated, my hand hovering near the knob. A strange, tight knot formed in my chest. I didn't want to engage; the effort of responding felt monumental. I took the flowers, the cool, waxy texture of the leaves feeling strange against my palm. I carried them into the hallway, the silence of the apartment pressing in around me. I placed them on the narrow console table, not looking at them, just standing there, arms crossed tightly over my chest, waiting for the moment to dismiss the intrusion.

I stared at them, the vibrant colors a jarring contrast to the grey monotony of my apartment. My jaw tightened, and I picked up a wilting stem, turning it over in my fingers as if searching for a hidden flaw. There was no note, just the weight of the petals and the silent, baffling gesture. I pushed the flowers aside, the movement jerky, and retreated to the living room, sinking onto the worn sofa. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, defeated weight settling onto my spine. I didn't look up when the doorbell rang again, the sound grating on my nerves.

I found the arrangement tucked beside my laptop, amidst the scattered papers and empty coffee mugs. The arrangement was offensively cheerful—bright orange tulips and a handful of stiff, white daisies. When I opened the door, the vibrant splash of purple and deep blue against the muted hallway seemed to demand attention. A cluster of exotic orchids, impossibly perfect, rested on the welcome mat.
